segments.paradiso-24-g41: S’elli is an archaic contraction of se + egli (“if he”); the clause is a conditional protasis. The three verbs ama, spera, crede are simple present indicatives coordinated by e, and the doubled bene is a rhetorical intensifier meaning “well, truly.” Word order is straightforward: conditional particle + subject + series of verbs.
segments.paradiso-24-g42: Non t’è occulto is a present declarative: non negates the predicate, t’ is the clitic for ‘to you’ placed before the finite verb è (common in older/poetic Italian), and occulto is the predicate adjective ‘hidden.’ The literal order corresponds to ‘it is not hidden to you.’
segments.paradiso-24-g43: Perché = ‘because’; ’l viso hai quivi = ‘you have there the viso’ where viso here means a kind of vision/gaze. Dov’ is the contracted relative ‘dove/in which’; ogne cosa dipinta is a nominative phrase (‘every painted thing’), and si vede uses the impersonal/passive si with vedere to mean ‘is seen.’
segments.paradiso-24-g44: Ma perché = ‘but because’; questo regno is the subject. Ha fatto is compound present perfect (‘has made’), and civi is an archaic plural meaning ‘citizens’ (object/complement of fatto). Per + la verace fede = ‘by/through the true faith’ giving the cause or means.
segments.paradiso-24-g45: A + infinitive expresses purpose or result here: a glorïarla = ‘to glorify her.’ The pronoun la is suffixed to the infinitive (glorïar‑la) and the dieresis on ï marks the vowel break for pronunciation (glor‑i‑ar‑la).
segments.paradiso-24-g46: Di lei parlare is an infinitival noun phrase ‘to speak of her.’ È ben is a fixed idiom meaning ‘it is fitting/right.’ Ch’ introduces the subordinate; arrivi is a present subjunctive (arrivi rather than indicative arriva) expressing the wished-for/resultative sense ‘that it may reach him,’ and a lui uses tonic prepositional phrase for ‘to him.’
segments.paradiso-24-g47: Sì come = 'just as' (sì = 'so/thus' + come = 'as'); the clause sets up a simile. Il baccialier is the subject 'the bachelor/young knight'; s’arma is the pronominal verb (si + arma) meaning 'arms himself' (reflexive) in the indicative present historically used to describe a habitual action; e non parla is simple present 'and does not speak'. Fin che = 'until' (conjunction), followed by the present indicative propone 'he/she/it proposes/puts (forward)' with l(maestro) as subject: 'until the master proposes the question.'
segments.paradiso-24-g48: per approvarla: per + infinitive (approvarla = approvare + clitic la) expresses purpose 'in order to approve/confirm it'; the clitic -la refers back to 'la question' (feminine) in the previous line.
segments.paradiso-24-g49: non per terminarla: non negates the following purpose phrase; per terminarla = 'in order to finish/end it' (terminarla = terminare + direct-object clitic la). This contrasts the master's aim (to approve, not to close the debate).
segments.paradiso-24-g50: così m’armava io d’ogne ragione: così = 'thus/so' linking back to the simile; m’armava is imperfect indicative of armare with enclitic m' (mi) = 'I armed myself' / 'I was arming myself' (a continuous or habitual past action); io is explicit subject for emphasis; d’ogne ragione = di + ogni 'with every reason/argument' (di = 'with/of', ogne = 'every', ragione = 'argument/reason').
segments.paradiso-24-g51: mentre ch’ella dicea: mentre = 'while' (temporal), ch’ = che (conjunction) introduces the subordinate clause; ella dicea is the imperfect 'she was saying' (dicea = diceva in modern orthography), so the timing is simultaneous — he readies himself while she speaks. Per esser presto a tal querente e a tal professione (next segment) completes the purpose.
segments.paradiso-24-g52: Dì is the imperative form of dire ('say'/'tell'), used as an interjection here 'Tell me' or 'Say'. (The provided sense list did not include this verbal sense, so the sense index is not available.)
segments.paradiso-24-g53: buon Cristiano = a direct address 'good Christian'; buon is the attributive adjective 'good' placed before the noun for emphasis, Cristiano is vocative here (addressing the listener).
segments.paradiso-24-g54: fatti manifesto: fatti is the imperative (second person singular) of farsi or a reflexive imperative (fa' + ti → fatti) meaning 'make yourself' or 'become' (here 'make yourself known'/'show yourself'); manifesto is an adjective 'manifest/clear/revealed' agreeing in sense with the implied reflexive object: 'make yourself manifest' or idiomatically 'make yourself known'.
segments.paradiso-24-g55: fede che è?: a terse interrogative. Fede = 'faith' (what creed or belief is meant); che here functions as the interrogative 'what' (colloquial/poetic inversion: 'what faith is it?'); è is the present indicative of essere 'is'. The phrase compresses 'What faith is it?' into archaic/poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-24-g56: This segment contains only a sentence-final punctuation mark (a period). It has no lexical or grammatical content and simply indicates the end of the preceding line or sentence.
segments.paradiso-24-g91: 'Ond'' is a contracted adverbial (from onde/onde) used as a discourse connector meaning 'whereupon' or 'thereupon'; 'io' is the nominative subject 'I' and together they introduce the reported speech (hence the colon).
segments.paradiso-24-g92: 'Sì' here is an affirmative/confirming adverb ('yes' or 'indeed'), and 'ho' is the first-person singular present of avere ('I have'); the direct object is omitted and understood from context (the coin already under discussion).
segments.paradiso-24-g93: Two parallel degree adverbs 'sì ... e sì ...' (= 'so ... and so ...') modify the adjectives 'lucida' and 'tonda'; both adjectives are feminine singular to agree with the implied feminine noun (la moneta/this thing). Word order places the degree adverb before each adjective for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-24-g94: 'Che' introduces a result/restrictive clause ('so ... that ...'); 'nel suo conio' literally 'in its stamping/coinage' (conio = the die or action of striking a coin); 'nulla' is the subject 'nothing'; 'mi s’inforsa' is an archaic reflexive construction (si + inforza) in which 'inforza' means 'forces/presses' and 'mi' is the dative/clitic marking the affected party (here the coin as the speaker's possession). Altogether: '...so ... that in its stamping nothing forces itself into me' (i.e. it takes no flaw/dent in the strike).
segments.paradiso-24-g95: 'Appresso' is an adverb meaning 'afterwards/next'; 'uscì' is the past-tense (perfect/simple past) third-person singular of 'uscire' ('issued forth/appeared'); 'de la' = 'della' ('from the'); and 'luce profonda' is the noun phrase 'profound/deep light'; the relative 'che lì splendeva' ('that shone there') modifies 'luce' with 'splendeva' in the imperfect to indicate ongoing past action.
segments.paradiso-24-g96: Reported speech: 'Questa cara gioia' = 'this dear/precious joy'; 'sopra la quale' is a preposition + relative pronoun 'upon which' (quale agreeing with the feminine noun 'gioia'); 'ogne virtù' = 'every/each virtue' (archaic form ogne for ogni); 'si fonda' is a reflexive/passive-like present ('is founded' or 'bases itself'), so the clause reads 'upon which every virtue is founded.'
segments.paradiso-24-g97: 'Onde' here functions interrogatively as 'whence/from where'; 'ti' is the dative clitic 'to you'; 'venne' is the third-person singular past of 'venire' ('came'). Together in the question form it asks 'whence came it to you?' or more naturally 'how/where did it come to you from?'
segments.paradiso-24-g98: This segment is punctuation only: a full stop marking the end of the preceding speech.
segments.paradiso-24-g99: E io is simply 'And I' (io = 'I'), introducing the speaker's reply; the conjunction e links this speech to what preceded.
segments.paradiso-24-g100: La larga ploia = 'the copious/downpouring rain' where larga qualifies ploia (a poetic variant of pioggia) as 'broad/abundant'. De lo Spirito Santo = 'of the Holy Spirit' (de = of; lo Spirito Santo = the Holy Spirit). The phrase images a plentiful outpouring of the Spirit.
segments.paradiso-24-g101: Ch’ è diffusa = 'which is spread' (ch’ = che = 'which'; è diffusa = 'is spread/poured out'). In su le vecchie e ’n su le nuove cuoia = 'upon the old skins and upon the new skins' (in/ su = 'on/upon', le vecchie/ le nuove cuoia = 'the old and the new skins' — an image alluding to parchments or to old and new covenants).
segments.paradiso-24-g102: È silogismo = 'is a syllogism' (i.e., a kind of conclusive reasoning); che la m’ha conchiusa = 'that has concluded/closed me' (la = 'it' or 'that [thing]', m’ = 'me', ha conchiusa = perfect of conchiudere 'has concluded/constrained'). Acutamente = 'acutely/sharply' and sì (here) = 'so' (intensifier): 'has so sharply concluded me'.
segments.paradiso-24-g103: Che ’nverso d’ella = 'that, compared to it / against it' (nverso = 'against/opposed to' or 'in comparison with'; d’ella = 'of it'). Ogne dimostrazion mi pare ottusa = 'every demonstration seems to me dull/obtuse' (ogne = 'every', dimostrazion = 'proof/argument', mi pare = 'seems to me', ottusa = 'dull/obtuse').
segments.paradiso-24-g1: A vocative address: "O sodalizio eletto" = "O elect fellowship." "Eletto" is a past-participle adjective post‑nominally modifying "sodalizio" (chosen/selected). The prepositional phrase "a la gran cena del benedetto Agnello" (a + definite article + noun + genitive) identifies the occasion/place: "at the great supper of the blessed Lamb."
segments.paradiso-24-g2: "Il qual" is a relative pronoun referring back to the Lamb ("which/who"). "Vi" is the indirect-object clitic meaning "to you." "Ciba" (from cibare) is a present-form verb meaning "feeds/nourishes" (3rd singular). "Sì" here begins the result clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-24-g3: Subordinate/result clause introduced by "che": "that your desire is always full." Note agreement: "vostra" modifies the feminine noun "voglia," and the predicate "piena" agrees in gender and number with "voglia." "È" is the present indicative of essere.
segments.paradiso-24-g4: "Se per grazia di Dio" is a conditional phrase meaning "if by the grace of God." "Questi" functions here as a demonstrative/pronominal subject "this one" (i.e., a person); "preliba" in the line functions as the verb "tastes/samples" (an archaic form) governing the following partitive phrase "di quel che cade de la vostra mensa" = "of what falls from your table."
segments.paradiso-24-g5: "Prima che" introduces a temporal clause and is followed by the subjunctive "prescriba" (3rd singular present subjunctive) because the action is anticipated/unrealized: "before death prescribes (assigns) his time." The clitic "li" is an enclitic referring back to the person(s)/thing(s) whose time is prescribed.
segments.paradiso-24-g6: "Ponete mente a" is an idiomatic imperative (second-person plural) meaning "pay attention to / give thought to." "L’affezione immensa" is the feminine noun phrase object. The verb "roratelo" is an imperative with the enclitic pronoun -lo: liturgically "let it be dewed/moistened" or figuratively "refresh it a little." "Alquanto" tempers the command: "a little/somewhat."
segments.paradiso-24-g7: "Voi bevete sempre del fonte" is literal: present indicative "you drink always from the source." "Del" is partitive/contracted preposition. "Onde" is an adverbial/relative use meaning "from which/whence" (archaic); "vien" is 3rd singular present of "venire" meaning "comes"; "quel ch'ei pensa" = "that which he thinks" ("ei" = "he").
segments.paradiso-24-g8: Short clause/marker: "Così Beatrice;" = "Thus said Beatrice" or "So Beatrice [spoke]." "Così" links back to what preceded and signals that Beatrice has just spoken.
segments.paradiso-24-g9: "E quelle anime liete" is the subject phrase "and those joyful souls." "Si fero spere" is a reflexive past action: archaic/poetic perfect meaning "made themselves spheres" ("si" reflexive + "fero" as an archaic/poetic form of 'fecero' or 'fecer[o]'). "Sopra fissi poli" is a locative phrase: "above the fixed poles."
segments.paradiso-24-g10: "Fiammando" is a present participle (or gerund) modifying the souls/spheres — "flaming/blazing." In poetry participles like this are often used adverbially.
segments.paradiso-24-g11: "A volte" is an adverbial phrase meaning "at times" or "sometimes," modifying the participle "fiammando" or the whole clause.
segments.paradiso-24-g12: "A guisa di comete" is a simile: "in the manner of/comparable to comets." "A guisa di" is a fixed poetic phrasing meaning "like/as."
segments.paradiso-24-g13: "E come" begins a simile "And as..." "Cerchi" here is a plural noun "rings/circles" (not the verb "to seek"); poetic word-order places the descriptive phrase "in tempra d’orïuoli" (in a setting of small gold ornaments) before the verb. "Si giran" is a reflexive/impersonal present: "they turn/are turned."
segments.paradiso-24-g14: "Che" introduces the result/observation clause: "that the first, to one who pays attention, seems quiet." "A chi pon mente" = "to whoever puts/turns his mind (to it)" i.e., "to whoever looks closely." "Pare" is present indicative meaning "seems/appears."
segments.paradiso-24-g15: Elliptical continuation of the previous comparison: "and the last (appears) that it flies." The verb "voli" (3rd singular present subj./indic.) is used in the sense "flies/appears to fly;" the copula from the prior clause is understood and omitted.
segments.paradiso-24-g16: "Così quelle carole" links the simile back to the scene: "So those caroles (round dances)." "Carole" is the plural of a medieval dance/song, here 'round-dances.'
segments.paradiso-24-g17: Poetically split adverb/adverbial phrase: "differente-mente danzando" = "differently dancing." The hyphenated split is merely a line break; together they form the adverb "differentemente" modifying "danzando."
segments.paradiso-24-g18: "De la sua ricchezza" is an ablative/causal phrase "from/by their richness." "Mi facieno stimar" is a causative/perception construction: "they made me estimate/judge (them)" — literally "they caused me to judge"; "facieno" is an archaic/plural present form "they made" and "stimar" is the infinitive "to estimate/judge."
segments.paradiso-24-g19: Short adjectival phrase "veloci e lente" (= "some swift and some slow") that completes what the speaker judged; adjectives agree in number with the plural subject/idea.
segments.paradiso-24-g20: "Di quella ch'io notai di più carezza" = "Of the one that I marked/noted as most full of affection/tenderness." "Vid' ïo uscire" is an archaic inversion: "vid' ïo" = "I saw" (vid' = vidi; ïo enclitic subject for emphasis), "uscire" = "to come forth/issue." "Un foco sì felice" = "a flame so fortunate/blessed/auspicious" — adjective used poetically.
segments.paradiso-24-g21: "Che" introduces the result clause: "so that none left it with more brightness." "Nullo" = "no one/none"; "vi" is locative/pronominal "in it/there" (referring to the flame or the place); "lasciò" is past tense "left/left behind"; "chiarezza" here means brightness/clarity (visual radiance).
segments.paradiso-24-g22: "E tre fïate intorno di Beatrice si volse" = "And three times it turned itself around Beatrice." "Tre fïate" = "three times/occasions"; "si volse" is the reflexive past/preterite of volgersi/volgere used here in poetic narrative sense "it turned/circled." "Con un canto tanto divo" modifies how it turned: "with so divine a song."
segments.paradiso-24-g23: "Che la mia fantasia nol mi ridice" = "which my imagination does not repeat to me." "Nol" is the contracted archaic combination of "non lo" ("not it/it not"); "ridice" (ridicere/ridire) here means "to tell again/repeat." The construction indicates the poet's inability to recall or render the song.
segments.paradiso-24-g24: Però is an adverb meaning 'therefore/so' linking this line to what precedes. Salta is 3rd-person singular present of saltare used idiomatically with la penna as subject: 'the pen skips' (i.e., fails to record). E non lo scrivo is a coordinate clause where scrivo is 1st-person singular present 'I write' (the subject 'I' is expressed by the verb); lo is the masculine direct object pronoun referring to the thing just perceived; the colon signals an explanatory clause to follow.
segments.paradiso-24-g25: Ché introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'); l' (elided article) + imagine nostra is the noun phrase 'our image' as subject. A cotai pieghe is a prepositional phrase: a here means 'at' or 'in' and cotai is an archaic demonstrative/adjective meaning 'such' modifying pieghe 'folds'.
segments.paradiso-24-g26: Non che here introduces a restrictive or concessive phrase often meaning 'not to mention' or 'much less'; l' is the article before parlare, which nominalizes the verb 'parlare' so that the phrase means 'not to speak of speaking' (i.e., speech itself).
segments.paradiso-24-g27: È is the 3rd-person singular present of essere 'is'. Troppo modifies the adjective phrase color vivo and functions as an adverbial intensifier 'too' (too much/too vividly). Color vivo is a nominal/adjectival phrase 'vivid color' (vivo here 'vivid/lively'), giving the predicate 'is too vividly colored'.
segments.paradiso-24-g28: O is the vocative interjection 'O/Oh' addressing someone. Santa suora mia is a vocative phrase 'my holy sister' (suora = nun/sister). Che introduces a relative clause 'who...' and sì ne prieghe divota describes her: sì 'so/so much', ne refers back to 'us' or 'of it/them' (i.e., in our behalf), prieghe here is plural 'prayers' or supplications, and divota is an adjective 'devout/devoted' modifying suora.
segments.paradiso-24-g29: Per lo tuo ardente affetto is a causal/means phrase 'by/through your ardent affection' (per + article + adjective + noun). Da quella bella spera mi disleghe is a request: disleghe is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person present-subjunctive (or optative) form 'may you unbind' used to ask or entreat someone to 'release me' (mi) from quella bella spera 'that beautiful sphere' (spera = celestial sphere).
segments.paradiso-24-g30: Poscia is an adverb 'afterwards/then'. Fermato is a past participle used adverbially/elliptically ('having stopped' or 'once it stopped'), creating a temporal participial phrase that sets the time for the following action.
segments.paradiso-24-g31: Il foco benedetto is the subject 'the blessed fire' (benedetto = 'blessed'). Dirizzò is passato remoto, 3rd-person singular of dirizzare 'directed/aimed'. A la mia donna is the indirect/prepositional phrase 'toward my lady' (Beatrice), and lo spiro is the direct object 'the breath' (spiro) that is being directed at her.
segments.paradiso-24-g32: Che is a relative pronoun 'which' referring back to lo spiro. Favellò is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of favellare 'spoke'. Così com' i' ho detto is a parenthetical 'thus/as I have said' (com' = com, i' = io contracted, ho detto = present perfect 'I have said'), linking this utterance to the narrator's prior report.
segments.paradiso-24-g33: Ed is a variant of e 'and', used before a vowel; ella is the subject pronoun 'she' that introduces direct speech. The colon marks the start of her speech.
segments.paradiso-24-g34: O luce etterna del gran viro is a vocative address: 'O eternal light of the great man'. A cui is a relative construction 'to whom' (a + cui), and Nostro Segnor lasciò le chiavi is a subordinate clause in passato remoto: 'Our Lord left/gave the keys to him' (alluding to St. Peter).
segments.paradiso-24-g35: Ch' is the contraction of che 'which/that'. Ei is an archaic subject pronoun 'he' (referring to the great man), portò is passato remoto 'carried/brought', and giù is the adverb 'down' — literally 'which he carried down'.
segments.paradiso-24-g36: Di questo gaudio is a prepositional phrase 'at/about this joy' (di + demonstrative + noun). Miro is 1st-person singular present of mirare used in the sense 'I marvel/admire' (here expressing wonder at the joy).
segments.paradiso-24-g37: Tenta is an imperative/request form (or present used as a command) of tentare 'test/try'; costui is the demonstrative noun 'this man' (the person to be tested). Di punti lievi e gravi is the phrase 'with light (easy) and heavy (serious) points/questions' specifying the manner of the test.
segments.paradiso-24-g38: Come ti piace is an idiom 'as you please/like': come 'as', ti the indirect-object pronoun 'to you', and piace is 3rd-person singular present of piacere 'it pleases (you)'.
segments.paradiso-24-g39: Intorno de la fede is a phrase meaning 'about/concerning the faith' (intorno 'about/around' + de la = archaic form of 'della' + fede 'faith').
segments.paradiso-24-g40: Per la qual is a relative phrase 'by/through which' (per + article + relative). Tu andavi is the imperfect tense 'you were going/you used to go' (indicating past action), and su per lo mare is an idiomatic expression 'up over the sea' (su = up, per lo mare = across/over the sea), describing the way in which the faith carried the addressee.
segments.paradiso-24-g74: E io appresso is an introductory phrase: E = 'and'; io = the first-person subject 'I'; appresso is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'after that' or 'next' (an older/poetic use). The colon that follows shows the speech that the speaker (I) is about to report; the verb of speaking is implied by the construction.
segments.paradiso-24-g75: This is a relative clause modifying 'Le profonde cose'. 'Le profonde cose' = 'the profound/deep things' (adjective before noun for emphasis). 'che' is the relative pronoun 'which/that'. 'mi' is an indirect-object clitic 'to me'. 'largiscon' is 3rd-person plural present of the archaic verb largire (here 'they give/grant'); subject is 'le profonde cose'. 'qui la lor parvenza' places the adverb 'qui' ('here') before 'la lor parvenza' ('their appearance/semblance') — a poetic order where 'la' (definite article) + 'lor' (poetic form of loro, 'their') precede 'parvenza'.
segments.paradiso-24-g76: 'a li occhi' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the eyes' (a + definite article li). 'di là giù' is a fused adverbial locution 'down there / yonder below' (poetic inversion of direction). 'son' is the contracted present 'sono' (3rd-person plural 'are'). 'sì ascose' = 'so hidden/secret' — 'sì' intensifies the adjective 'ascose'. Word order places the locative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-24-g77: 'che' introduces a result/explanatory clause: 'that ...'. 'l’esser' is a nominalized infinitive (l' + essere) meaning 'the being/existence'. 'loro' is the possessive 'their' modifying 'esser'. 'v’è' is the locative 'vi è' contracted to 'v’è' meaning 'there is' or 'exists there'. 'in sola credenza' = 'only in belief' (sola = 'only/merely'). Together: 'that their being there is only in belief.'
segments.paradiso-24-g78: 'sopra la qual' = 'upon which' (sopra + relative 'la qual', referring back to 'credenza'). 'si fonda' is a reflexive/passive construction meaning 'is founded' or 'finds its foundation' (si marks the intransitive/reflexive/passive use). 'l’alta spene' = 'the lofty hope' — spene is an older form for 'speranza' and the adjective alta precedes it for emphasis; semantically: 'upon which lofty hope is founded.'
segments.paradiso-24-g79: This final clause links by consequence: 'e però' = 'and therefore'. 'di sustanza' is a genitive/source phrase 'from substance/essence'. 'prende' is 3rd-person singular present of prendere ('takes' / 'derives'). 'intenza' is a noun (intention/aim/meaning). The literal construction is 'and therefore takes intent from substance' = 'therefore it derives its intent from substance.'
segments.paradiso-24-g80: 'E da questa credenza' = 'and from this belief' (da marks source/premise). 'ci convene' is an impersonal/necessity construction: convenire here means 'it is necessary for us' or 'we must'. 'silogizzar' is an archaic infinitive (silogizzare) without the final -e meaning 'to syllogize' or 'to reason by syllogism'; the infinitive is the complement of convene.
segments.paradiso-24-g81: 'sanz’' is the contracted form of 'sanza' (without). It governs the infinitive phrase 'avere altra vista' = 'having any other sight/vision'. 'altra vista' means 'another kind of vision' (i.e. direct, immediate sight instead of inferential knowledge). The colon closes the conditional explanatory clause.
segments.paradiso-24-g82: 'però' = 'therefore'. 'intenza' (intention/aim) is the object to be retained. 'd’argomento' = 'of argument' or 'for argument' (specifying the kind of intent). 'tene' is an imperative/command form here ('hold/keep'), an older/poetic form addressing the addressee to maintain that 'intention' while proceeding; syntactically it closes the speech with a directive.
segments.paradiso-24-g83: 'Allora' = 'then' (temporal adverb). 'udi’' is the poetically shortened past tense 'udii' meaning 'I heard' (final -i is often dropped or apostrophized in Dante). Together this introduces reported speech that follows the colon.
segments.paradiso-24-g84: This conditional begins with 'Se' = 'if'. 'quantunque' here means 'whatever' / 'however much' and scopes over the clause. 's’acquista' is the impersonal/passive 'si acquista' ('is acquired' or 'one acquires'), written with elision 's’'. 'giù' = 'down/below' (adverb), and 'per dottrina' = 'by doctrine/teaching' — indicating that what is learned below by teaching is under consideration.
segments.paradiso-24-g85: 'fosse' is the imperfect subjunctive of essere ('were'); subjunctive here expresses the counterfactual condition ('if ... were so understood'). 'così' = 'thus'. '’nteso' is a contracted form of 'inteso' ('understood'): initial i dropped and marked by apostrophe, a common poetic elision.
segments.paradiso-24-g86: This is the apodosis (result clause). 'non' negates; 'lì' = 'there' (referring to 'down below'); 'avria' is the conditional 'would have' (archaic form corresponding to modern 'avrebbe'). 'loco' = 'place/room' (meaning 'no occasion/need/room'). 'ingegno di sofista' = 'the wit/skill of a sophist' (sofista = sophist/specious reasoner). The structure 'non lì avria loco X' = 'there would be no place for X.'
segments.paradiso-24-g87: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' and modifies the verb spirò. Spirò is 3rd-person singular past tense (passato remoto) of spirare here meaning 'breathed' or 'sighed' (poetic/older sense). Di quello amore acceso links the action to its cause: di = 'from/of', quello = 'that', amore acceso = a past-participial adjective phrase 'love set aflame' or 'burning love'.
segments.paradiso-24-g88: Indi is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon' (here used to link the speech), but the supplied sense list does not include that meaning so its sense index is not available. Soggiunse is the passato remoto 3rd-sing. of soggiungere, meaning 'he added' (said in addition).
segments.paradiso-24-g89: Assai bene is an adverbial phrase intensifying the following verb (assai = 'very/much', bene = 'well'). È trascorsa is present perfect/simple past sense (è + past participle here idiomatically = 'has passed' or 'has long since passed away') with trascorsa as past participle of trascorrere. D’esta moneta = 'of this coin'; già = 'already/long since'; la lega e ’l peso = 'the alloy (composition) and the weight' of the coin — lega here is a noun meaning alloy/composition (not the verb 'legare').
segments.paradiso-24-g90: Ma = 'but' introducing a question. Dimmi is the imperative (tell me). Se introduces an indirect yes/no question 'whether/if' (the provided sense list lacks this meaning, so index is unavailable). Tu is the explicit subject 'you'. L’hai = combination of the direct object clitic l' (= 'it') + hai (2nd-person singular present of avere) = 'do you have it'. Ne la tua borsa = 'in your purse' (ne here meaning 'in/of it' or 'in it' referring to the coin and la tua borsa = 'your purse/bag').
segments.paradiso-24-g104: Io udi' poi is a compact, archaic past narration: Io = I (first person). Udi' is the poetical/passé-remote form of udire (here 'I heard'), written with an apostrophe showing elision of a final vowel; poi is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. The colon marks that direct speech follows.
segments.paradiso-24-g105: L'antica e la novella proposizion is the noun phrase 'the ancient and the new proposition' with L' and la as the feminine definite articles before antica/novella; antica/new are coordinate adjectives. The following che così ti conchiude is a relative clause modifying proposizion: che = 'that', così = 'thus/so' modifying the verb conchiude (present indicative, 3rd sing.), and ti is the clitic object 'you' receiving the action 'concludes/encloses'.
segments.paradiso-24-g106: Perché opens the direct-question 'why'. L' (here the clitic standing for 'it' or the article referring back to 'proposizion') combines with hai (second-person present of avere) to mean 'do you have/regard (it)'. The clitic order l'hai is a typical enclitic contraction. Tu is an emphatic subject pronoun; per in this phrase means 'as'/'for' (i.e. 'regard as'), and favella is an archaic word for 'speech/words'.
segments.paradiso-24-g107: This segment is a single punctuation mark (the closing period of the verse/question). It has no lexical items and simply ends the preceding line of direct speech.
segments.paradiso-24-g108: E io: is the short formula 'And I:' introducing the speaker's reply; E = 'and', io = 'I'. The colon introduces the quoted answer.
segments.paradiso-24-g109: La prova che 'l ver mi dischiude is 'the proof that discloses the truth to me.' La prova = 'the proof'; che introduces the relative clause; 'l ver is an elided form of 'il ver' = 'the truth' (l' attached before ver); mi is the indirect object 'to me'; dischiude is present indicative (3rd sing.) of dischiudere = 'opens/reveals'.
segments.paradiso-24-g110: Son l'opere seguite, is a copular clause with inversion: son = sono 'are' (here agreeing with the predicate); l'opere = 'the works'; seguite is a past participle/adjective 'that follow' or 'ensuing' agreeing with opere. Dante often places the verb before the noun-phrase for metrical or emphatic reasons.
segments.paradiso-24-g111: a che natura non scalda ferro mai né batte incude is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by a che = 'for which/whereby'. Natura is the subject 'nature'; non ... mai = 'never' (negation + mai); scalda ferro = 'heats iron' (scalda present indicative); né batte incude = 'nor strikes an anvil' (batte present indicative; incude = anvil, nominative). The clause asserts that such works are not made by smithing (no heating or hammering).
segments.paradiso-24-g112: Risposto fummi is an archaic/periphrastic expression: risposto = 'answer' (past participle) and fummi is the contracted sequence fu + mi (mi fu) giving a passive/experiential sense 'an answer was given to me' — functionally here 'I answered'. Dante often uses such inversion of auxiliary + clitic.
segments.paradiso-24-g113: Dì is the imperative form of dire ('say' / 'tell'), written here with stress and introducing a direct request to speak. In older spelling the apostrophe may mark the missing vowel: di' = 'say'.
segments.paradiso-24-g114: chi t'assicura che quell' opere fosser? = 'who assures you that those works were (so)?' Chi = 'who'; t' is clitic for 'to you' (dative/indirect object); assicur a is present indicative 'assures/guarantees'; che introduces the subordinate clause; quell' opere = 'those works'; fosser is the archaic subjunctive/imperfect form of essere (modern fossero), here expressing reported/dubious past — 'were' or 'had been'. The question challenges the addressee's warrant for claiming the works are such.
segments.paradiso-24-g57: Ond' is a poetic/archaic adverb (from 'onde') meaning 'thereupon' or 'from there', introducing the action; 'io levai' is passato remoto first-person singular ('I raised/lifted') with the direct object 'la fronte' (the brow/forehead). The prepositional phrase 'in quella luce' gives direction/place ('into that light'), and the relative/adverbial clause 'onde spirava questo' uses 'onde' = 'from which' plus the imperfect 'spirava' ('was emanating') with 'questo' ('this') as the element being emanated, a typical Dantean inversion where the relative adverb precedes the verb.
segments.paradiso-24-g58: 'Poi' is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards'; 'mi volsi' is passato remoto, reflexive, first-person singular of 'volgere/volgersi' meaning 'I turned (myself)'; the preposition 'a' governs the person turned to, 'Beatrice'. The order verb + subject pronoun ('volsi' then 'io' omitted here) is narrative and emphatic.
segments.paradiso-24-g59: 'Ed' = 'and'; 'essa' is a feminine pronoun referring back to Beatrice; the adjective 'pronte' agrees with the plural noun 'sembianze' ('ready/quick'); 'sembianze femmi' is a noun phrase meaning 'womanly/feminine appearances or features' (the noun 'femma' used adjectivally/epithetically). 'Perch’' (= perché) introduces a purpose/result clause and takes the imperfect subjunctive 'ïo spandessi' ('that I might pour/spread'), where 'ïo' is emphatic 'I'; 'l’acqua di fuor del mio interno fonte' is a complex noun phrase meaning 'the water from out of my inner spring' (di fuor = 'from out of', del mio interno fonte = 'of my inner source').
segments.paradiso-24-g60: ‘La Grazia’ is the subject ('the Grace'); 'che mi dà' is a relative clause ('that gives me' or 'that grants me'); the following 'ch’ io mi confessi' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' with the present subjunctive 'confessi' ('that I may confess' or 'that I should confess'), expressing what the grace effects or grants.
segments.paradiso-24-g61: 'Comincia’ io' is a narrative tag: the verb (here contracted/truncated in the text) is first-person past tense ('I began') with the subject pronoun optionally placed after the verb for emphasis or cadence; the apostrophe marks elision typical of Dante's orthography.
segments.paradiso-24-g62: 'Da l’alto primipilo' is a prepositional phrase 'from the high primipilo' indicating source or origin invoked in the speaker's prayer; 'primipilo' is an obscure/technical epithet (here left as a noun denoting a primary source/guide), and 'alto' ('high/lofty') qualifies it.
segments.paradiso-24-g63: 'Faccia' is the third-person singular present subjunctive of 'fare' used optatively/jussively ('may [it] make' or 'let [it] make'); the direct object 'li miei concetti' ('my concepts/thoughts') is followed by the predicative past participle 'bene espressi' ('well expressed'), where 'espressi' agrees in gender/number with 'concetti'.
segments.paradiso-24-g64: 'E seguitai' is narrative: 'and I continued' (passato remoto 'seguitai'), introducing the quoted speech that follows.
segments.paradiso-24-g65: 'Come' ('as') introduces a clause reporting authority: '’l verace stilo ne scrisse' — 'the true style/pen wrote of it'. The clitic 'ne' refers back to the matter just mentioned ('of it/about it'), and 'scrisse' is passato remoto third-person singular ('wrote').
segments.paradiso-24-g66: 'Padre' is a vocative 'father', addressing the person being spoken to; set off by commas as direct address.
segments.paradiso-24-g67: 'Del tuo caro frate' is a genitive phrase 'of your dear brother'; the relative clause 'che mise teco Roma nel buon filo' uses 'mise' (passato remoto of 'mettere'/'riporre') meaning 'he put/placed' with 'teco' ('with you') as accompaniment and 'nel buon filo' ('in the good thread') as a figurative locative/placement phrase. Word order follows Dantean narrative style.
segments.paradiso-24-g68: This is a propositional definition: 'fede è sustanza di cose sperate e argomento de le non parventi' — 'faith is the substance of things hoped for and the argument (proof/reason) of things not yet appearing.' 'È' is the copula; 'sustanza' and 'argomento' are predicate nouns joined by 'e'. 'Sperate' is a past participle used adjectivally, 'non parventi' ('not appearing') is an archaic present participial adjective.
segments.paradiso-24-g69: 'E questa pare a me' = 'and this seems to me'; the predicate 'sua quiditate' is possessive + noun: 'its quiditate' (an abstract noun here meaning something like 'settledness' or 'determined nature'). 'Pare a me' is the impersonal 'seem(s) to me' construction.
segments.paradiso-24-g70: 'Allora udi’' — 'then I heard' (passato remoto first-person 'udii' / 'udi' in Dante), introducing the reported words that follow; 'allora' situates the moment in the narrative.
segments.paradiso-24-g71: 'Dirittamente' = 'directly/straightforwardly'; 'senti' here is an imperative or admonitory present ('hear!/listen!/understand!') addressed to the listener (second-person singular). The phrase functions as an instruction to pay attention and understand plainly.
segments.paradiso-24-g72: 'Se bene intendi' is a conditional clause ('if you understand well'); 'intendi' is second-person singular present ('you understand' / 'you perceive'). 'Perché la ripuose' is an indirect question/explanatory clause introduced by 'perché' ('why'/'because') with 'ripuose' (past tense, third-person) meaning here 'he placed/put it' (the object 'la' refers back to the thing being discussed). 'Tra le sustanze' ('among the substances') is the location of that placement.
segments.paradiso-24-g73: Continuation: 'e poi tra li argomenti' = 'and then among the arguments'; 'poi' = 'then/afterwards', 'tra' = 'among', and 'li argomenti' names the second category into which the thing was placed. The phrase completes the explanatory instruction.
segments.paradiso-24-g161: This is the opening of a simile: Come = 'as'. '’l segnor' is the definite article + noun ('the lord'); 'ch’ ascolta' is a relative clause ('who listens') with the truncated che (ch’) before the present indicative ascolta. 'quel che i piace' = 'that which pleases him/that pleases him' — literally 'that which to-him pleases'; the internal 'quel che' functions as 'what/that which'.
segments.paradiso-24-g162: 'da indi' here functions adverbially as 'thereupon'/'from there' and links the relative clause to the subsequent action. 'abbraccia' is present indicative 'embraces' and takes 'il servo' as direct object; word order (verb before article+noun) is normal in Dante's poetic line to keep metre.
segments.paradiso-24-g163: Gratulando is a gerund ('congratulating'/'rejoicing'), indicating the manner of the embracing; it modifies the main verb abbraccia. 'per la novella' is a prepositional phrase 'on account of/for the news'. The gerund + per-phrase shows simultaneous action and reason.
segments.paradiso-24-g164: Tosto che is an idiom meaning 'as soon as'. The cliticized subject 'el' (archaic for 'egli') plus reflexive si with tace (present indicative) yields 'as soon as he is silent' or 'as soon as he falls silent'.
segments.paradiso-24-g165: Così is a simple adverb ('thus'/'so') that completes the simile and introduces the corresponding action on the poet (i.e., 'so' / 'thus' in the main clause).
segments.paradiso-24-g166: Benedicendomi is a gerund formed from benedire plus the clitic -mi, meaning 'blessing me' (simultaneous action). Cantando is a second gerund 'singing'; together they indicate the manner in which the following action is performed: 'blessing me and singing'.
segments.paradiso-24-g167: 'Tre volte' = 'three times' (adverbial numeral). Cinse is the passato remoto (simple past) of cingere ('encircled/embraced'); the object pronoun me follows it for poetic order, giving 'thrice encircled me'.
segments.paradiso-24-g168: Sì com' = 'just as' / 'even as' (linking back to the simile). 'Io tacqui' uses tacqui, the passato remoto first person of tacere ('I fell silent/I was silent'), emphasizing a completed action simultaneous with the encircling.
segments.paradiso-24-g169: The noun phrase 'l’appostolico lume' = 'the apostolic light' (l' + adjective + noun). 'al cui comando' = 'at/to whose command' (cui = 'whose/to whom', comando = 'command/authority'). 'Io avea detto' is a compound past (imperfect of avere + past participle) used in narrative as a pluperfect-ish form: 'I had said'.
segments.paradiso-24-g170: Sì here is an emphatic 'thus'/'so'. 'Nel dir' = 'in saying' or 'when speaking' (nel = in + il; dir = infinitive used as noun). 'Li' is an archaic dative clitic meaning 'to him' and piacqui is passato remoto first person singular of piacere: 'I pleased (him)'. The whole exclamation reports what the speaker had said.
segments.paradiso-24-g115: Quel medesmo is a demonstrative phrase meaning "that very same one" (quel + medesmo). The relative pronoun che introduces a clause modifying quel medesmo. Vuol is the third-person singular of volere in the present indicative (vuol = vuole) meaning "wants"; provarsi is a pronominal infinitive (provare + si) meaning "to prove/test oneself." The whole segment is a noun phrase + relative clause: "that very same one who wants to prove himself."
segments.paradiso-24-g116: Non is the simple negation "not." Altri here is a pronoun/adjective meaning "others" or "another (person)"; together the fragment means "not another / not others." It is an emphatic corrective inserted between clauses.
segments.paradiso-24-g117: Il here is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun (used where modern Italian would have egli/egli/egli) introducing the verb; ti is the second-person clitic object pronoun "to you"; giura is the third-person singular present indicative of giurare, "(he) swears/promises." Word order is subject pronoun + clitic object + verb: "He swears to you."
segments.paradiso-24-g118: Se is the conditional conjunction "if." ’l is the elided article/definite article il before mondo (here representing "the world" meaning humankind). Si rivolse is the passato remoto (rivolse) with reflexive si: "turned itself/was turned (to)" — here "turned to Christianity/converted." Al is the contraction of a + il; cristianesmo = "Christianity/Christendom."
segments.paradiso-24-g119: Diss' io is an inverted, archaic way to say "I said" (dissi/disse with clitic elision). Diss' is a past tense form of dire; io is the emphatic subject pronoun. The inversion places the verb before the pronoun for emphasis: "I said."
segments.paradiso-24-g120: Sanza is the archaic form of senza, meaning "without." Miracoli is the plural noun "miracles/wonders." The phrase sanza miracoli qualifies the preceding conditional: "if ... without miracles."
segments.paradiso-24-g121: Quest' uno (quest' = questo elided + uno) = "this one." È tal: è is present tense of essere "is," tal (short for tale) means "such." Together: "this one is such."
segments.paradiso-24-g122: Che introduces a clause of result/comparison: "that." Li altri uses the archaic plural article li + altri = "the others." Non sono is the present of essere with negation: "are not." Il centesmo is literally "the hundredth (part)" — idiomatically here "not even the hundredth (in worth/importance)."
segments.paradiso-24-g123: Ché is a causal conjunction meaning "for/because." Tu intrasti is passato remoto of intrare, "you entered" (subject pronoun tu is emphatic). Povero e digiuno are adjectives modifying the subject: "poor and fasting" (digiuno = fasting/hungry). In campo is a locative phrase "into the field" or "in the field," here metaphorical for going out to work/mission.
segments.paradiso-24-g124: A seminar is an infinitival phrase with a (a + infinitive) meaning "to sow." La buona pianta is the direct object: "the good plant." Che introduces a relative clause: fu già vite — fu is passato remoto of essere "was," già = "formerly/once," vite here is "vine" (poetic meaning), and ora è fatta pruno: ora = "now," è fatta = passive/become (fatta from fare used as "has become"), pruno = "thornbush/plum-bramble." So: "to sow the good plant which once was a vine and is now become a thornbush."
segments.paradiso-24-g125: Finito is a past participle used as an absolute construction meaning "when/after this was finished" or "having finished this." Questo is the demonstrative "this" referring back to the preceding matter. Together: "When this was finished."
segments.paradiso-24-g126: L'alta corte santa is the subject noun phrase "the high holy court" (l' = the, alta = lofty/high, corte = court, santa = holy). Risonò is the passato remoto of risonare "resounded/echoed." Per le spere means "through the spheres" (spere = celestial spheres). Un 'Dio laudamo' is the exclamation quoted; here laudamo functions as a verb-form meaning "we praise" in a liturgical formula. Ne la melode = "in the melody/chant" (ne = in), che là sù si canta is a relative clause "which up there is sung," with si making the verb canta impersonal/passive: "that is sung up there."
segments.paradiso-24-g127: E = "and." Quel baron = "that nobleman/leader" (demonstrative + noun). Che introduces a relative clause. Sì di ramo in ramo is an adverbial phrase meaning "thus/from branch to branch," expressing movement along the branches (a poetic image); the repetition di ... in ... marks successive motion.
segments.paradiso-24-g128: Essaminando is a present participle (gerund) meaning "examining" or "questioning"; it qualifies the action of the baron, indicating he was examining as he moved.
segments.paradiso-24-g129: Già is "already." Tratto is the past participle of trarre (to draw/pull); m’avea (mi aveva) is the auxiliary construction (avea + past participle) meaning "had drawn me" — here the clitic me appears attached before the auxiliary in archaic order (tratto m'avea). The whole means "who had already drawn me along."
segments.paradiso-24-g130: Che begins this subordinate clause linking to the previous ("who... that"). A l'ultime fronde = "to/at the outermost leaves" (a + l' + ultime fronde). Appressavamo is the first-person plural imperfect of appressare/appressarsi: "we were approaching" or "we drew near." The imperfect gives a continuous setting contemporaneous with the earlier action.
segments.paradiso-24-g131: Ricominciò is the passato remoto of ricominciare, "he began again/ resumed." It marks a new speech act following the descriptive clause.
segments.paradiso-24-g132: La Grazia = "The Grace" (article + proper abstract noun; here Grace as a theological/charitable power). This is the subject of the ensuing speech.
segments.paradiso-24-g133: Che introduces a relative clause modifying La Grazia. Donnea is an archaic verb (donneare) meaning "to court/to woo/act in a winning/enticing way"; con la tua mente = "with/through your mind" (i.e., by affecting your intellect). The phrase means "which courted/affected you through your mind."
segments.paradiso-24-g134: La bocca t'aperse: literal word order is "the mouth opened to you" (la bocca = subject, t' = clitic indirect object 'to you,' aperse = passato remoto of aprire 'opened'); idiomatically "it opened your mouth" (i.e., gave you the ability to speak). Infino a qui = "up to here/this point." Come aprir si dovea is an impersonal/obligational clause with inverted word order: aprir (infinitive) + si (impersonal reflexive) + dovea (imperfect/conditional sense of dovere) = "as it ought to be opened."
segments.paradiso-24-g135: Sì ch’io = "so that I" (sì = so/thus; ch’ = che). Approvo is present indicative "I approve/endorse." Ciò che fuori emerse = "that which came forth/appeared outwardly" (fuori emerse is passato remoto "emerged/came out"). The clause states the speaker's approval of what was manifested externally.
segments.paradiso-24-g136: Ma or = "but now." Convien is an impersonal present verb (convenire) meaning "it is necessary/it is fitting." Espremer (infinitive) literally "to squeeze out" is used figuratively here as "to extract/express." Quel che credi = "that which you believe" (quel = that; che = which; credi = present of credere, 'you believe').
segments.paradiso-24-g137: E = "and." Onde is a relative/adverb meaning "whence/from which." A la credenza tua = "to your belief" (a + la + credenza tua). S’offerse is the passato remoto (si offerse) of offersi (to offer oneself/ to be offered); the reflexive s' indicates the middle voice — here "was offered" or "presented itself." The clause: "and from which it was offered to your belief."
segments.paradiso-24-g138: A vocative address. O is the interjection (=“O/Oh”) calling out; santo padre is the noun in vocative (holy father). The comma marks direct address to the addressee.
segments.paradiso-24-g139: Coordination continuing the address: e (and) links another addressee; spirito is a second vocative noun. che vedi is a relative clause (che = “who/that”) with vedi in the present indicative (you/one/you (sing.) see), modifying spirito. ciò che credesti sì is a nested clause: ciò = “that which,” che = relative “which,” credesti is passato remoto 2nd‑person singular “you believed,” and sì is an intensifying adverb “so/thus.”
segments.paradiso-24-g140: This clause continues the content of what was believed. The initial che is a connective “that”; tu is the explicit subject; vincesti is passato remoto 2nd‑person singular (“you prevailed/overcame”). ver’ lo is a contracted/poetic form (ver’ = verso/toward) before the article lo; sepulcro is the direct object ‘tomb’; più giovani piedi is a post‑nominal modifier: più + giovane = ‘more young’ → ‘younger feet’ (poetic way to speak of quicker/younger steps).
segments.paradiso-24-g141: comincia’ io is an inverted, contracted past form: comincia' represents cominciai (passato remoto “I began”), with the subject pronoun io placed after the verb for emphasis — a common poetic inversion.
segments.paradiso-24-g142: Tu vuo’ is the colloquial/poetic present of volere (“you want”). ch’io introduces a subordinate clause with the subjunctive manifesti (present subjunctive, because volere triggers a subjective subordinate). La forma is the direct object; qui = “here/at this point”; del pronto creder mio is a genitive phrase: del = of the, pronto modifies creder (creder used substantively = “belief”); mio is the possessive ‘my’ placed after the noun in older usage.
segments.paradiso-24-g143: A final short clause: e anche = “and also”; la cagion di lui = “the reason/cause of it/him” (di lui refers back to the thing being asked about); chiedesti is passato remoto 2nd‑person singular “you asked.” The whole clause reiterates that the addressee asked for the reason as well.
segments.paradiso-24-g144: E io rispondo is a simple present declarative: E = “and”; io is the subject pronoun; rispondo is present indicative 1st‑person singular “I answer.” The colon marks the reported statement that follows.
segments.paradiso-24-g145: Io credo is emphatic (subject pronoun present). in uno Dio uses uno before Dio to emphasize unity; solo ed etterno uses ed (old form of e before vowel) and the adjective etterno (archaic spelling) — an attributive string “alone and eternal.”
segments.paradiso-24-g146: che introduces a relative clause modifying Dio (“who”); tutto ’l ciel = entire heaven (tutto + definite article ellipted to ’l for metrical reasons); move is 3rd‑person singular present indicative “moves” (God is described as the mover of the heavens).
segments.paradiso-24-g147: non moto is an adjectival phrase contrasting with move: non = not; moto here functions as a participial adjective “moved,” so non moto = “not moved” (i.e., unmoved mover).
segments.paradiso-24-g148: con amore e con disio is a pair of prepositional complements; con = “with,” followed by abstract nouns amore and disio (archaic spelling of desiderio) giving the manner/inner motive: “with love and with desire.”
segments.paradiso-24-g149: e a tal creder non ho io pur prove is a negative construction: a tal creder = “to such belief”; non ho io = emphatic negation with subject pronoun after verb (“I do not have”); pur here means “only/merely” or “even” depending on nuance; prove fisice e metafisice = “physical and metaphysical proofs” (plural adjectives agreeing with prove).
segments.paradiso-24-g150: ma dalmi anche la verità che quinci piove per Moïsè: ma = “but”; dalmi is a compressed/archaic expression here functioning like “gives me” or “to me comes” (poetic; form is irregular); anche = “also”; la verità = the truth; che quinci piove = relative clause “that from here pours/comes down” (quinci = “from here/this place” in older usage); per Moïsè = “through Moses.”
segments.paradiso-24-g151: per profeti e per salmi: a simple prepositional listing: per = “through/by/through the medium of,” profeti = “prophets,” salmi = “psalms”; the repeated per emphasizes each channel of revelation.
segments.paradiso-24-g152: per l’Evangelio e per voi che scriveste poi che l’ardente Spirto vi fé almi: per l’Evangelio = “through the Gospel”; per voi = “through you” (addressing the writers); che scriveste = relative clause “who wrote” (passato remoto, 2pl); poi che introduces a temporal clause “after”; l’ardente Spirto = “the ardent Spirit” (Holy Spirit); vi fé almi = “made you inspired/gracious” (vi = in you; almi = life‑giving/gracious, older adjective).
segments.paradiso-24-g153: e credo in tre persone etterne: e = and; credo = I believe (present); in tre persone = “in three persons” (Trinity language); etterne = archaic spelling of eterne/eterne = “eternal.”
segments.paradiso-24-g154: e queste credo una essenza sì una e sì trina: e = and; queste = “these” (the three persons); credo una essenza = “I believe [them to be] one essence” (credo takes a direct object clause here, omitted verb understood); sì ... e sì ... = emphatic repetitions “so one and so three,” trina = “threefold/trinitarian.”
segments.paradiso-24-g155: che soffera congiunto ‘sono’ ed ‘este’: che = relative “that”; soffera is an older/fossilized future or modal sense from sofferare meaning “will/does endure/permit/contain” (here: “so that it bears”); congiunto = “joined/together”; ‘sono’ ed ‘este’ are quoted verb‑forms (sono = “I am/they are” modern Italian; este is an older form). The clause says that the one essence can sustain the joined expressions “sono” and “este.”
segments.paradiso-24-g156: De la = archaic/dialectal equivalent of della (of the/about the); profonda condizion divina = noun phrase with two adjectives, “profound divine condition”; ch’io tocco mo is a relative clause: ch’io = that I; tocco is present indicative “I touch/mention”; mo = now/just now (poetic adverb).
segments.paradiso-24-g157: la mente mi sigilla più volte l’evangelica dottrina: la mente = “the mind”; mi is the dative/indirect object “to me”; sigilla is present indicative “seals” (puts a seal on, i.e., confirms/locks in); più volte = “several times/many times”; l’evangelica dottrina = the Gospel‑teaching (subject of the clause). Word order places subject after the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-24-g158: Quest’ è ’l principio: Quest’ is the clitic form of questo (this); è is present indicative “is”; ’l principio = the beginning (article elided to fit the metre). The phrase is a simple identificational statement.
segments.paradiso-24-g159: quest’ è la favilla che si dilata in fiamma poi vivace: quest’ è la favilla = “this is the spark”; che si dilata = relative clause with si as reflexive/passive marker and dilata (present indicative) “expands/widens”; in fiamma poi vivace = “into a flame, afterwards lively” (poi = then/afterwards; vivace = lively).
segments.paradiso-24-g160: e come stella in cielo in me scintilla: e = and; come = “as/like”; stella in cielo = “a star in heaven”; in me = “within me/to me”; scintilla is present indicative 3rd‑person singular “it sparkles/twinkes” (or “gives a spark”). The image compares the growing faith to a star shining inside the speaker.

sentences.paradiso-24-s9: If he loves well and hopes well and believes well, it is not hidden from you, for you have there the sight in which every painted thing is seen; but because this realm has made (him) a citizen through the true faith, in order to glorify her, it is right that speaking of her reach him.
sentences.paradiso-24-s10: Just as the young bachelor arms himself and says nothing until the master puts the question — to approve it, not to finish it — so I armed myself with every argument while she was speaking, in order to be ready for such a questioner and such a declaration.
sentences.paradiso-24-s11: “Tell me, good Christian, make yourself known: what faith is it?”
sentences.paradiso-24-s21: Whereupon I said: "Yes, I have it, so bright and so round that in its minting nothing forces itself into me."
sentences.paradiso-24-s22: Then there issued forth from the profound light that shone there: "This dear joy upon which every virtue is founded, whence came it to you?"
sentences.paradiso-24-s24: And I: "The copious shower of the Holy Spirit, which is poured out upon the old skins and upon the new skins, is a syllogism that has so acutely concluded me that every demonstration seems dull compared with it."
sentences.paradiso-24-s1: O elect fellowship at the great supper of the blessed Lamb, who feeds you so that your desire is always full; if by the grace of God this one tastes of what falls from your table before death prescribes his time, pay attention to the immense affection and let it be moistened a little: you always drink from the source whence comes that which he thinks.
sentences.paradiso-24-s2: Thus Beatrice; and those joyful souls made themselves spheres above the fixed poles, flaming at times like comets.
sentences.paradiso-24-s3: And as rings in a setting of little golden ornaments turn so that the first appears quiet to one who pays attention, and the last to be flying; so those caroles, dancing differently, made me judge from their richness — some swift and some slow.
sentences.paradiso-24-s4: Of the one that I noticed as most affectionate I saw a flame come forth so blessed that none left it with greater brightness; and three times it turned about Beatrice with a song so divine that my imagination does not repeat it to me.
sentences.paradiso-24-s5: So the pen skips it and I do not write it: for our image, at such folds — not to speak of speech — is too vividly colored.
sentences.paradiso-24-s6: O my holy sister, who so devoutly prays for us, by your burning affection free me from that fair sphere.
sentences.paradiso-24-s7: Then, after pausing, the blessed fire directed its breath toward my lady, which spoke thus as I have said.
sentences.paradiso-24-s8: And she: 'O eternal light of the great man to whom Our Lord left the keys — which he carried down — I marvel at this joy; put this man to the test with light and heavy points, as you please, concerning the faith by which you went up over the sea.'
sentences.paradiso-24-s17: And I then replied: “The profound things that here grant me their appearance are so hidden to the eyes down below, that their being there exists only in belief, upon which lofty hope is founded; and therefore they derive their intent from substance.”
sentences.paradiso-24-s18: And from this belief we must reason by syllogism, without having any other sight; therefore keep (hold) the intent of an argument.”
sentences.paradiso-24-s19: Then I heard: “If whatever is acquired down below by teaching were thus understood, there would be no place there for the wit of a sophist.”
sentences.paradiso-24-s20: Thus he sighed from that burning love; then he added: "The alloy and the weight of this coin have long since gone by; but tell me whether you have it in your purse."
sentences.paradiso-24-s25: I then heard: "Why do you take the old and the new proposition that so encloses you for divine speech?"
sentences.paradiso-24-s27: And I said: "The proof that reveals the truth to me is the works that follow, for which nature never heats iron nor strikes an anvil."
sentences.paradiso-24-s28: I answered: "Tell me, who assures you that those works were so?"
sentences.paradiso-24-s13: Thereupon I lifted my brow into that light from which this was emanating; then I turned to Beatrice, and she assumed ready, womanly looks so that I might pour out the water from my inward spring.
sentences.paradiso-24-s14: "The Grace that grants me to confess," I began, "may, from the high primipilo, make my thoughts well expressed."
sentences.paradiso-24-s15: And I continued: "As the true pen wrote of it, father, concerning your dear brother who placed Rome with you in the good thread, faith is the substance of things hoped for and the argument of things not appearing; and this seems to me its settled nature."
sentences.paradiso-24-s16: Then I heard: "Hear plainly, if you well understand why he placed it among the substances, and then among the arguments."
sentences.paradiso-24-s37: As the lord who listens to what pleases him, and thereupon embraces the servant, congratulating him on the news as soon as he falls silent; so, blessing me and singing, the apostolic light thrice encircled me — even as I was silent — at whose command I had said: 'thus, in speaking, I pleased him!'
sentences.paradiso-24-s29: He swears to you that it is that very same one who wants to prove himself, not another.
sentences.paradiso-24-s30: 'If the world turned to Christianity,' I said, 'without miracles, this one is such that the others are not even a hundredth: for you entered the field poor and fasting to sow the good plant which once was a vine and is now made a thornbush.'
sentences.paradiso-24-s31: When this was finished, the high, holy court resounded through the spheres with a 'Dio laudamo' in the melody that is sung up there.
sentences.paradiso-24-s32: And that nobleman, who, examining from branch to branch, had already drawn me along as we were approaching the outermost leaves, began again: "The Grace, which courted with your mind, opened your mouth to this point as it ought to be opened, so I approve what came out; but now it is necessary to press out (express) what you believe, and from what was offered to your belief."
sentences.paradiso-24-s33: “O holy father, and spirit who sees that which you so believed — that you prevailed toward the sepulchre with younger feet,” I began; “you want me to reveal here the form of my prompt belief, and you also asked the reason for it.
sentences.paradiso-24-s34: And I answer: I believe in one God, alone and eternal, who moves the whole heaven — not moved — with love and with desire; and for such belief I do not have only physical and metaphysical proofs, but I also have the truth that here pours down to me through Moses, through prophets and psalms, through the Gospel and through you who wrote after the ardent Spirit made you inspired; and I believe in three eternal persons, and I believe these to be one essence, so one and so three, such that the conjoined ‘sono’ and ‘este’ are borne together.
sentences.paradiso-24-s35: Of the profound divine condition that I have just touched upon, the evangelical doctrine seals my mind many times over.
sentences.paradiso-24-s36: This is the beginning, this is the spark that then widens into a lively flame, and, like a star in heaven, it twinkles within me.
